nativeRange.habitat: Found in backwaters, floodplains, and slow-moving or still tributaries of the upper Amazon basin and Araguaia River, typically in areas with dense aquatic vegetation.

decorPreferences: dense planting along sides and back

diet.notes: In the wild, the Penguin Tetra is a micropredator — an active hunter of small prey — feeding on invertebrates, insects, larvae, and crustaceans. In aquaria, it generally accepts a wide range of foods including quality flakes and micro-granules, freeze-dried and frozen bloodworms, Daphnia, brine shrimp, and tubifex.

imageCandidates.notes: Kkonstan; 2124x1414. Crisp, well-lit lateral view of a vibrant live specimen showing the diagnostic bold oblique black stripe extending into the lower caudal lobe and the characteristic slight head-up posture.
Juan R. Lascorz; 3111x2074. Sharp high-res side profile, silvery body with clean black oblique stripe running into the lower tail lobe; different individual/background for variety.
Brian Gratwicke; 1024x683. Sharp, colourful live specimen against green planting, black oblique stripe clearly extending into the caudal fin.

summary: The Penguin Tetra is a peaceful schooling fish from the Amazon basin, recognized by its bold oblique black stripe and distinctive head-up swimming posture. Generally considered a good choice for beginners when kept in groups of six or more.
careNotes: Best kept in groups of at least 6, with 10 or more preferred — fin-nipping behavior is significantly reduced in properly-sized schools. The Penguin Tetra tends to swim at a distinctive head-up, oblique angle and spends much of its time near the surface. It is closely related to Thayeria obliqua (the Hockey Stick Tetra), which carries a shorter black stripe that does not extend into the lower caudal lobe; the two are sometimes confused in the trade. Despite tolerating a fairly wide pH range, the Penguin Tetra is sensitive to elevated ammonia, nitrite, and nitrate, making good filtration and regular water changes strongly advisable. The species tends to do best in densely planted aquaria with open swimming lanes in the center; adding Indian almond leaves or dried botanicals can help recreate the natural tannin-stained water of its home range.
breedingNotes: Reported to breed fairly readily in captivity. A dedicated spawning tank with soft, slightly acidic water and a mesh liner or spawning mop is generally advised. Females may produce several hundred to several thousand eggs per spawn, and parents have been known to eat the eggs, so they should be removed after spawning. Eggs tend to hatch within 12–24 hours, with fry typically becoming free-swimming within 3–4 days. First foods are infusoria or a commercial fry powder, graduating to microworms and brine shrimp nauplii as the fry grow.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Used as primary; Aquarium Co-Op (designated primary for fish-freshwater slice) has no dedicated Penguin Tetra care page.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (3.6 cm SL / 6 cm TL), temperature (22–28°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (5–19 dH), and native range. All high-stakes fields confirmed consistent with SeriouslyFish.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Additional cross-check. Temperature 22–28°C, pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 5–20 dKH, size 3.8–7.6 cm, tank 20–30 gal (76–114 L), lifespan 3–5 years, min group 6. Also noted fin-nipping tendency in small groups.
